name: Model and Generation Effects Baseline
summary: The staged model-effects baseline finds that synthetic student response quality is sensitive to model choice and generation workflow, but the evidence does not support a single cross-task model ranking. Reported effects include bounded LLM-version sensitivity in Agent4Edu, model- and prompt-specific IRT alignment in NAEP proxy-student simulation, post-training improvements over prompting in tutoring-dialogue simulation, multi-model and ensemble effects for LLM respondents in item evaluation, current preprint evidence that history-conditioned profiles plus DPO/GRPO improve turn-level simulated-student fidelity, six-model science idea comparisons, few-shot versus fine-tuned/multi-agent/DPO simulated-learner workflows, IRT-aligned DPO for item difficulty, and cognitive-prototype programming simulations.
main_blockers: The first pass is targeted rather than systematic.
Compared studies vary by response format, subject, grade band, model family, prompt, post-training workflow, metric, and comparator, so model effects are not directly rankable across sources.
Several comparisons change model and workflow together; causal isolation is limited unless later studies align tasks, prompts, decoding settings, and metrics.
The newest history-conditioned profile evidence is a May 2026 arXiv preprint and should be monitored for revision.
best_current_signals: Agent4Edu reports a bounded GPT-4 exploratory condition above GPT-3.5-turbo under a 100-learner setup, while also documenting a profile/memory/reflection/action workflow.
Srivatsa et al. compare 11 LLMs and grade-enforcement prompts on NAEP items and conclude that no tested model-prompt pair reliably simulates average real students across subjects and grades.
Scarlatos et al. report that SFT and DPO student simulators outperform prompting on several dialogue fidelity metrics, while prompting can inflate correctness via majority-class correct responses.
Liu et al. show model-specific respondent distributions and item-calibration correlations, with no single LLM matching human variability and mixed evidence for ensemble augmentation.

Duan et al. report that history-conditioned profiles, simulator DPO, and profile-generator GRPO improve simulated student turn fidelity over prompting-only and simpler fine-tuning baselines in a current preprint.
Nguyen and Cao compare six LLMs on open-ended science ideas and report model- and grade-level variation across realism metrics.
Cao et al. show that fine-tuning, multi-agent, and DPO workflows improve mathematics simulated-learner authenticity over few-shot prompting, with different tradeoffs.
SMART and Wu et al. add workflow evidence for IRT-aligned DPO and cognitive-prototype/self-refinement approaches outside essay scoring.
interpretive_note: Model and generation effects should be expressed as task-bound sensitivity claims. A stronger model, a fine-tuned simulator, or a profile-conditioned workflow may improve one metric while still failing another, and prompt-only correctness can be misleading when generated responses overfit majority-class correct answers.
limitations: The staged source set is a compact bootstrap baseline, not an exhaustive review.
Evidence spans personalized learning response records, NAEP multiple-choice items, College Algebra item calibration, and math tutoring dialogues.
The claim does not establish that synthetic responses can replace real student responses for validation, item calibration, or scorer benchmarking.
Prompt-engineering-only evidence is intentionally deferred to the adjacent prompt-engineering-effects node unless it also compares broader model or workflow choices.
update_notes: Srivatsa et al. per-model Table 2, Table 4, and Table 5 values were extracted in the 2026-06-02 numeric follow-up; check whether Agent4Edu provides fuller controlled GPT-4 comparisons outside the small exploratory condition before model-ranking synthesis.
Track later versions or venue status for Duan et al. because it is current preprint evidence.

A later bundle should explicitly separate model-family effects, post-training effects, history/profile conditioning effects, decoding/sampling effects, prompt-engineering effects, and task-specific comparator effects.
2026-06-03 claim-hardening update added published validity-condition findings from Nguyen and Cao, Cao et al., SMART, and Wu et al.

supporting_evidence.label: Bounded LLM-version sensitivity
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Agent4Edu provides bounded evidence that LLM version can affect learner-response simulation performance, but not enough for a general cross-task model ranking.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Agent4Edu reports GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 ACC rows, with the GPT-4 result tied to a 100-learner exploratory setup.
limitations: The model comparison is exploratory and sample-condition bound.
The agent workflow and model version are not fully separable.

supporting_evidence.label: Model-prompt ability alignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Proxy-student ability alignment varies by model and prompt, and no tested model-prompt pair is reliable across the NAEP conditions.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Srivatsa et al. test 11 LLMs and multiple grade-enforced prompts on NAEP response patterns using IRT.
limitations: The evidence is multiple-choice item-response ability alignment, not dialogue or essay realism.
The extracted table values still do not justify ranking models across unrelated response-generation tasks.

supporting_evidence.label: Post-training versus prompting
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Post-trained student simulators improve several tutoring-dialogue fidelity metrics over prompting, though correctness and error metrics remain nuanced.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Scarlatos et al. compare prompted GPT methods with SFT and DPO Llama simulators and report stronger results for post-training on acts, knowledge, linguistic similarity, ROUGE-L, and tutor-response metrics.
limitations: The comparison is a method-package comparison, not a pure model-family causal test.

supporting_evidence.label: Multi-model respondent and ensemble effects
supporting_evidence.conclusion: LLM respondent behavior differs by model, and ensemble/resampling can improve some psychometric similarity signals while remaining mixed.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Liu et al. compare six LLMs and augmentation strategies against human College Algebra response distributions and item calibration.
limitations: The item set is a single College Algebra lesson.
Narrow LLM proficiency distributions limit human-substitution claims.

supporting_evidence.label: History-profile and RL workflow effects
supporting_evidence.conclusion: History-conditioned profiles and RL-tuned workflow stages can improve simulated-student turn fidelity in current preprint evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Duan et al. compare prompting, SFT/DPO, history SFT, ProfileRL, and ablations over profile sections and RL stages.
limitations: The source is a current arXiv preprint.
The evaluated dataset is private and math-domain specific.

supporting_evidence.label: Controlled model and workflow comparisons
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The newer validity-condition studies add direct evidence that model and workflow choices matter, while reinforcing that effects remain task- and comparator-bound.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Nguyen and Cao compare six LLMs on science idea realism; Cao et al. compare few-shot, fine-tuned, multi-agent, and DPO simulated-learner workflows; SMART uses IRT-aligned DPO for item-difficulty prediction; and Wu et al. use cognitive prototypes and self-refinement to improve imperfect programming response simulation.
limitations: The studies vary by response format, comparator, metric, and workflow.
The support strengthens sensitivity claims, not a universal model ranking.

applies_when: The claim is framed as task-bound sensitivity to model and workflow choices.
does_not_apply_when: The claim is used as a universal model ranking across subjects, response types, or stakes levels.
unknown_or_unreviewed_contexts: Aligned head-to-head studies that hold task, prompt, decoding, model, and comparator constant.
